q: A camera is an optical instrument that forms and records an image of an object.
The image in a camera can only be recorded on film and cannot be detected by an electronic sensor.
Regardless of how the image is recorded, all cameras form images in the same basic way.
A convex lens focuses the light from the object to form a real inverted image on the film or electronic sensor.
A diaphragm is placed behind the convex lens to control the amount of light energy falling on the film.
The aperture is a circular opening through which the light passes.
The size or the diameter of the aperture is expressed in terms of $f$-numbers.
The $f$-number of a camera is the ratio of the focal length of the lens to the diameter of aperture.
The larger the $f$-number, the wider the aperture is.
When taking a photograph in bright light, a large $f$-number (narrow aperture) must be used.
When taking a photograph in dim light, a large $f$-number must be used.
A shutter controls the time during which light enters the camera through the lens.
The shutter speed is expressed in terms of the exposure time.
The shorter the exposure time, the higher the shutter speed is.
A focusing ring allows for the adjustment of the distance between the lens and the film to obtain a sharp image.
The film consists of a thin layer of chemical compounds of silver.
Chemical reaction occurs when light energy falls on the film, forming an image of an object.
In a digital camera, the electronic sensor is made up of millions of tiny pixels which respond to light.
The human eye consists of sense organs which are capable of receiving visual images and sending information to the brain.
The eyelid opens and closes the eye, making it similar to the lens cover of a camera.
The cornea is the front portion of the eye and is covered by a tough, transparent membrane.
The region between the cornea and the eyelens is filled with a transparent liquid called the vitreous humour.
The region between the eyelens and the retina contains a transparent liquid called the vitreous humour.
The iris is the colored portion of the eye and controls the amount of light energy entering the eye.
The pupil is located in the centre of the iris and light passes through it.
The iris adjusts the size of the pupil in the eye.
The pupil automatically dilates in dim light and contracts in bright light.
Ciliary muscles are attached to the eyelens and can vary its thickness.
When near objects are viewed, the ciliary muscles relax.
When distant objects are viewed, the ciliary muscles relax and the eyelens becomes less convex.
The retina is the light sensitive area of the cells at the back of the eye.
The retina consists of rods and cones.
The yellow spot on the retina consists of rods exclusively.
The yellow spot is the most light-sensitive spot on the retina.
When an image is formed at the blind spot, it cannot be seen at all.
The optic nerve enters the eye at the yellow spot.
The cornea, aqueous humour, eyelens, and vitreous humour are all refracting substances.
The image of an object formed on the retina is real, inverted, and larger than the size of the object.
Farsightedness is also known as hyperopia.
Nearsightedness is also known as myopia.
In farsightedness, nearby objects are seen clearly, but distant objects are blurry.
For a person with farsightedness, the rays from near objects intersect at a point behind the retina.
Farsightedness can be corrected by the use of spectacles with diverging lens or concave lens.
Nearsightedness is a defect of vision in which near objects are seen clearly, but objects far away are blurry.
For a person with nearsightedness, the rays from distant objects intersect at a point in front of the retina.
Nearsightedness can be corrected by the use of spectacles with diverging lens or concave lens.
The least distance of distinct vision for people with normal vision is about $25 cm$.
For normal vision, the maximum distance of distinct vision is infinity.
When objects at infinity are viewed, the eyes are strained and cannot be viewed for a long time.
A microscope is an optical instrument which uses lenses to make enlarged images of objects too small for the unaided eye.
A compound microscope has at least two convex lenses: an objective lens and an eyepiece lens.
The lens nearer the object in a compound microscope is called the eyepiece.
The objective lens produces an image which is real, inverted, and larger than the size of the object.
The final image formed by a compound microscope is virtual and on the same side as the object.
The magnifying power ($MP$) of a compound microscope is the sum of the magnification of the objective and the magnifying power of the eyepiece.
For a large magnifying power, both the objective and the eyepiece should have short focal lengths.
The focal length of the objective should be shorter than that of the eyepiece in a compound microscope.
Electron microscope employs electron beams in place of light and uses electron lenses.
Optical microscopes have a better magnification than the electron microscope.
The electron microscope can reach a magnifying power of $1,000,000$.
Optical microscopes use electrostatic or electromagnetic lenses.
Electron microscopes use electrons which have shorter wavelengths that allow greater magnification.
The two main types of electron microscope are the Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) and the Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM).
In a Transmission Electron Microscope, the accelerated electron beam is focused by electrostatic or electromagnetic lenses.
An optical instrument used for viewing distant objects is called a telescope.
Two types of telescope are the astronomical telescope and the terrestrial telescope.
The refracting astronomical telescope consists of an objective of long focal length and an eyepiece of short focal length.
Both lenses in a refracting astronomical telescope are convex lenses.
Galileo invented a refracting telescope.
The final image formed by the eyepiece in an astronomical telescope is real and erect.
To obtain sharp images of distant objects, the objective of an astronomical telescope should have a large diameter.
Most telescopes used at astronomical observatories use a convex lens as the objective.
The reflecting telescope was first suggested by Newton.
In the terrestrial telescope, a concave lens is used as an eyepiece to produce an erect final image.
Total reflecting prisms are used in binoculars to reduce the length of the terrestrial telescope.
Binoculars consist of a pair of telescopes, one for each eye.
Optics is the branch of physics that studies the nature and properties of light.
LASER is an acronym for Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation.
A laser is a source of a narrow beam of intense, monochromatic, coherent light.
Lasers can only produce light in the visible part of the electromagnetic radiation.
A continuous laser beam can range in power from a fraction of a milliwatt to more than a megawatt.
Lasers are classified into five main types, including fiber lasers and dye lasers.
A laser contains three essential elements: laser medium, power supply, and optical cavity.
The process of supplying energy to the laser medium to excite it to emit light is called pumping.
The optical cavity concentrates light to stimulate the emission of laser radiation.
The optical cavity consists of two highly reflective mirrors, one of which allows some light to come out as a laser beam.
Two essential processes to initiate a laser action are stimulated emission and population inversion.
In population inversion, the population of the upper level of a transition is lower than that of the lower level.
Coherence means that the light waves a laser contains are in phase with one another.
Lasers are used in bloodless surgery and destroying kidney stones.
Laser light is used to collect information from the bar code printed on products.
Fibre optics works on the principle of total internal reflection.
An optical fibre is composed of two concentric layers called the core and cladding.
The refractive index of the core of an optical fibre is slightly smaller than that of the cladding.
Total internal reflection occurs if the angle of incidence at the core to cladding interface is greater than the critical angle.
The light beam in an optical fibre attenuates and dies off over a certain distance due to refraction and absorption.
Endoscopy is a surgical procedure used to examine the interior of a hollow organ.
Endoscopes use optical fibres to observe an image inside the body.
A spectrometer is a scientific instrument used to separate and measure spectral components of a physical phenomenon.
An optical spectrometer consists of a light source, collimator, dispersion elements, and a telescope.
Angle of dispersion information can be used to determine the wavelength and chemical make up of a source.
A dispersion element such as a prism or grating is used to disperse a collimated beam.
Photometry is the science of measuring visible light in units that are weighted according to the sensitivity of the human eye.
The human eye responds only to light having wavelengths between about $400 nm$ and $700 nm$.
The human eye is more sensitive to green light of $555 nm$ compared to other colors.
Luminous flux ($\\Phi$) is a measurement of the power of visible light produced by a light source.
One lumen ($1 lm$) is equivalent to $683$ watts of light with a wavelength of $555 nm$.
Luminous intensity ($I_v$) is a measure of the luminous flux emitted by a light source per unit solid angle.
The candela ($cd$) is the SI basic unit for luminous intensity.
In the SI system, a solid angle is expressed in steradian ($sr$).
The solid angle subtended at the centre by the whole surface area of a sphere is $2\\pi$ steradian.
Luminance ($L_v$) is the luminous intensity per unit area of light travelling in a given direction.
The SI unit for luminance is candela per metre squared ($cd \\cdot m^{-2}$).
Illuminance ($E_v$) is the total luminous flux incident on a surface per unit area.
The SI unit for illuminance is lux ($lx$).
One lux is equal to one lumen per metre squared ($1 lm \\cdot m^{-2}$).
The minimum average illuminance for reading areas in school study halls should be $500 lux$.
The inverse square law of illuminance states that illuminance is directly proportional to the distance $r$.
